On this page

  • Chain Rule for \(w_2\)
  • Plugging in the Values
  • The Simplification
  • Code Correlation
import numpy as np

We can effectively write the Neural Network as: Hidden Layer Neurons (Linear Combination):

Neuron 1: \(z_1 = w_{11}x_1 + w_{21}x_2\)

Neuron 2: $z_2 = w_{12}x_1 + w_{22}x_2$2.

Matrix Form Representation:The operations above are equivalent to the following matrix multiplication:\[\begin{bmatrix} z_1 \\ z_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} w_{11} & w_{21} \\ w_{12} & w_{22} \end{bmatrix} \times \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}\]Output Calculation (Pre-activation):The sum entering the final node is calculated as:\[z_{out} = (w_{31} \cdot \text{ReLU}(z_1)) + (w_{32} \cdot \text{ReLU}(z_2)) + b_{out}\]Final Output:The final prediction \(y\) is obtained by applying the Sigmoid activation:\[y = \text{Sigmoid}(z_{out})\]

# Activation Functions
def sigmoid(x):
    return 1 / (1 + np.exp(-x))

def sigmoid_derivative(x):
    return sigmoid(x) * (1 - sigmoid(x))

def relu(x):
    return np.maximum(0, x)

def relu_derivative(x):
    return (x > 0).astype(float)
### Lets write the activation functions

def sigmoid(x):
  return 1/(1+np.exp(-x))

def sigmoid_derivative(x):
  return sigmoid(x)*(1-sigmoid(x))

def relu(x):
  return np.maximum(0,x)

def relu_derivative(x):
    return (x > 0).astype(float)
##Input > 0: Derivative is $1$ (gradient passes through).Input $\le$ 0: Derivative is $0$ (gradient is blocked).
## Lets define the NN Class

\[\frac{\partial Loss}{\partial z_2} = \underbrace{\frac{\partial Loss}{\partial a_2}}_{\text{Loss Gradient}} \times \underbrace{\frac{\partial a_2}{\partial z_2}}_{\text{Sigmoid Derivative}}\]\[\frac{\partial Loss}{\partial z_2} = \left( \frac{a_2 - y}{a_2(1 - a_2)} \right) \times \left( a_2(1 - a_2) \right)\]

Chain Rule for \(w_2\)

\[ \frac{\partial \text{Loss}}{\partial w_2} = \underbrace{\frac{\partial \text{Loss}}{\partial a_2}}_{\text{Loss Gradient}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{\partial a_2}{\partial z_2}}_{\text{Sigmoid Derivative}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{\partial z_2}{\partial w_2}}_{\text{Hidden Input}} \]

Plugging in the Values

\[ \frac{\partial \text{Loss}}{\partial w_2} = \underbrace{\left( \frac{a_2 - y}{a_2(1 - a_2)} \right)}_{\text{Error}} \cdot \underbrace{\left( a_2(1 - a_2) \right)}_{\text{Sigmoid Slope}} \cdot \underbrace{a_1}_{\text{The Signal}} \]

The Simplification

The sigmoid terms cancel out, leaving a clean formula:

\[ \frac{\partial \text{Loss}}{\partial w_2} = (a_2 - y) \cdot a_1 \]

Code Correlation

  • \((a_2 - y)\) → dz2
  • \(a_1\) → self.a1
class SimpleNeuralNetwork:
  def __init__(self,input_size=2,hidden_size=2,learning_rate=0.1,epochs=100):
    self.w1=np.random.randn(input_size,hidden_size)
    self.b1 = np.zeros((1, hidden_size))
    self.w2=np.random.randn(hidden_size,1)
    self.b2=np.zeros((1,1))
    self.lr=learning_rate
    self.epochs=epochs


  def forward_pass(self,X):
    self.z1=np.dot(X,self.w1)+ self.b1
    self.a1=relu(self.z1)
    self.z2=np.dot(self.a1,self.w2)+ self.b2
    self.a2=sigmoid(self.z2)
    return self.a2

  def compute_loss(self,y_true,y_pred):
    return -np.mean(y_true*np.log(y_pred+1e-8)+(1-y_true)*np.log(1-y_pred+1e-8))


  def backward_pass(self,X,y_true,y_pred):
    m=X.shape[0] ###computing number of records in X
    dz2=y_pred-y_true
    dw2=np.dot(self.a1.T,dz2)/m
    dz1 = np.dot(dz2, self.w2.T) * relu_derivative(self.z1)
    dw1 = np.dot(X.T, dz1) / m
    db1 = np.sum(dz1, axis=0, keepdims=True) / m
    db2 = np.sum(dz2, axis=0, keepdims=True) / m

    # Update weights and biases
    self.w2 -= self.lr * dw2
    self.b2 -= self.lr * db2
    self.w1 -= self.lr * dw1
    self.b1 -= self.lr * db1


  def fit(self, X, y):
        self.loss_history = []
        for i in range(self.epochs):
            y_pred = self.forward_pass(X)
            loss = self.compute_loss(y, y_pred)
            self.loss_history.append(loss)
            self.backward_pass(X, y, y_pred)
            if i % 100 == 0:
                print(f"Epoch {i}, Loss: {loss:.4f}")

  def predict(self, X):
        probs = self.forward_pass(X)
        return (probs > 0.5).astype(int)

# Create dataset
np.random.seed(0)
X = np.random.rand(100, 2)
y = (X[:, 1] > X[:, 0]).astype(int).reshape(-1, 1)  # 1 if y > x else 0

# Initialize and train model
nn = SimpleNeuralNetwork(epochs=1000)
nn.fit(X, y)

# Predict on a few points
test_points = np.array([[0.2, 0.8], [0.9, 0.1], [0.4, 0.5]])
predictions = nn.predict(test_points)
print("Predictions:", predictions)
Epoch 0, Loss: 0.6452
Epoch 100, Loss: 0.4378
Epoch 200, Loss: 0.3089
Epoch 300, Loss: 0.2341
Epoch 400, Loss: 0.1883
Epoch 500, Loss: 0.1579
Epoch 600, Loss: 0.1368
Epoch 700, Loss: 0.1212
Epoch 800, Loss: 0.1092
Epoch 900, Loss: 0.0996
Predictions: [[1]
 [0]
 [1]]
# --- PLOTTING SECTION ---
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.plot(nn.loss_history, color='royalblue', linewidth=2)
plt.title('Model Loss Over Epochs', fontsize=14)
plt.xlabel('Epoch', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('Loss (MSE)', fontsize=12)
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)
plt.show()
